title: Coin Change
problem_statement: You are given an integer array coins representing coins of different denominations and an integer amount representing a total amount of money. Return the fewest number of coins that you need to make up that amount. If that amount of money cannot be made up by any combination of the coins, return -1. You may assume that you have an infinite number of each kind of coin.

examples.explanation: You cannot make 3 with coins of 2.

intuition: This is an unbounded knapsack problem where we need to find the minimum number of items (coins) to reach a target sum, with unlimited supply of each item.

approaches.name: Recursive Brute Force
approaches.description: For each coin, decide to use it or not, recursing on the remaining amount. Base: if amount == 0, return 0; if amount < 0 or no coins left, return infinity.
approaches.why_not: Exponential time due to exploring all combinations. Fails for amount > 20 or so.
interview_commentary: This approach tries every possible combination of coins. For each coin, I can choose to include it (subtract from amount) or skip it.
The recursion depth is up to amount, and at each level, I branch into two choices, leading to exponential time.
This will work for small amounts but TLE for larger ones due to overlapping subproblems.

approaches.name: Memoized Recursion (Top-Down DP)
approaches.description: Same recursion, but memoize on (amount, index) to avoid recomputing subproblems.
approaches.why_not: Still recursive, but bottom-up is iterative and can be more space-efficient.
interview_commentary: To optimize, I'll memoize on the state (remaining amount, current coin index).
This reduces time to O(amount * n) where n is number of coins, since each state is computed once.
Space is O(amount * n) for the memo table.

approaches.name: Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming
approaches.description: Use a dp array where dp[i] is the minimum coins for amount i. Initialize dp[0]=0, others to infinity. For each coin, update dp[i] = min(dp[i], dp[i-coin] + 1).
approaches.why_not: This is the standard optimal solution for coin change.
interview_commentary: Bottom-up: start from 0 and build up to amount. For each coin, iterate through all amounts >= coin and update the minimum.
This ensures we consider all coins for each amount, and since coins are unlimited, we can use the same coin multiple times.
Time is O(n * amount), space O(amount).

interview_qa.a: Because amount+1 is larger than any possible coin count (since max coins would be amount/1 = amount), so it acts as infinity for min comparisons.

interview_qa.q: What if coins include 0 or negative values?
interview_qa.a: The problem assumes positive coins, but if 0, it would cause infinite loop; negative would make it unbounded.

interview_qa.q: How does this differ from coin change II (number of ways)?
interview_qa.a: Coin change II counts combinations, this counts minimum coins. DP is similar but tracks count vs min.
Only if coins are canonical (like US currency), otherwise DP is needed for correctness.
